content.full_text:
Re
Fern PorrrrcAI- Pnacrrcgs C oMMrssroN
P.O. Box 807 . 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
December 19,2001
Phil Trounstine
620 Middlefield Drive
Aptos, CA 95003
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-01-254
Dear Mr. Trounstine
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").l This letter should not be construed as advice on any
conduct thatmay have already taken place. Finally, our response is based on the facts
presented. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity.
Qn re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)
QUESTTON
1. Does activity involved in seeking Power Purchase Agreements constitute
lobbying requiring registration as a lobbyist, or is it in the nature of procurement that
does not necessitate registration as a lobbyist?
2. Does the one-year prohibition apply to the Governor's former communications
Director even though the Power Authority did not exist at the time he was employed?
3. Is the Power Authority under the direction and control of the Governor's
office?
CONCLUSION
1. No. Your activities in securing contracts do not comprise an attempt to
influence legislative or administrative action.
I Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commissionregulations appearatTitle 2, sections
18109-18997, of tlte California Code of Regulations. All citations are to the Governmerfi Code unless
indicated otherwise.


File No A-01-254
Page No. 2
2. Yes
3. Yes. The Power Authority is a state administrative agency subject to the
direction and control of the Governor.
F'ACTS
You were communications director for Governor Gray Davis from January 1999
through mid-June 2001. You are now an independent communications consultant. You
have recently been asked by a potential client to represent their interest in obtaining
power purchase agreements from the California Consumer Power and Conservation
Financing Authority ("Power Authority").
ANALYSIS
I. Lobbvist Status
Section 82039 defines a lobbyist as
"'Lobbyist'means any individual who receives two thousand dollars
($2,000) or more in economic consideration in a calendar month,
other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, or whose
principal duties as an employee are to communicate directly or
through his or her agents with any elective state official, agemcy
official, or legislative offrcial for the purpose of influencing
legislative or administrative action. No individual is a lobbyist by
reason of activities described in Section 86300."
"Influencing legislative or administrative action" is defined as "communicating
directly or taking any other action for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting,
influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing arly legislative or
administrative action." (Reg 18239, subd. (d)(a).) We assume, forpurposes of our
analysis here, that the Power Authority is a state agency.
"Administrative action" is defined in section 82002 as the proposal, drafting,
development, consideration, amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of any
rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-legislative
proceeding. Pursuant to Regulation 18202, subdivision (a)(6), a proceeding of a state
agency at which an action is taken awarding a grant or contract is not a quasi-legislative
proceeding for the purposes of Section 82002. Hence, the provisions of the Act reaching
lobbyist activities do not reach actions regarding a grant or contract. (See Cordia Advice
Letter, No. A-99-235; Rewerts Advice Letter, No. 4-94-098.) Accordingly, your
activities do not constitute lobbying requiring registration as a lobbyist.


File No. A-01-254
Page No. 3
II. Post-Governm ent-Employment Restrictions
Does the one-year prohibition apply if the Power Authority was not in existence
during your tenure? Is the Power Authority under the "direction and control" of
the Governor's office?
This aspect of your letter concerns post-employment issues, colloquially known
as the "revolving door" prohibition and the permanent ban on "switching sides." The Act
places certain restrictions on individuals who have recently left state service and who
wish to use the expertise and relationships they developed at their former agency for
compensation by third persons. However, you have only asked questions regarding the
one-year ban.2
Specifically, you ask whether the one-year prohibition applies to you "given that
[the] power authority did not exist at the time [you were] employed." You also ask
whether the Power Authority is under the direction and control of the Governor's ofiice.
Section 87406, subdivision (dxl), of the Act provides, in pertinent part, that no
officer or designated employee of a state administrative agency:
"[F]or a period of one year after leaving office or employment,
shall, for compensation, act as agent or attorney foq or otherwise
represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before
any state administrative agency, or officer or employeethereof,for
which he or she worked or represented duringthe 12 months before
leaving office or employment, if the appearance or communication
is made for the purpose of influencing3 administrative or legislative
action,a or influencing any action or proceeding involving the
issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license,
grafit, or contract, or the sale or purchase ofgoods or property. For
pu{poses of this puagraph, an appearance before a state
' This letter does not address issues pertaining to the permanent ban on switching sides ($$
8'7400-87402,Reg. 18741.1)andtheprohibitiononinfluencingprospectiveemployment($87407,Reg.
t8747).
3 "Influencing legislative or administrative action" includes inlluencingby any means, including
but not limited to the provision or use of informatiorl statistics, or analyses. ($ 82032.) "Administative
action" is defined in section 82002 as the proposal, drafting dwelopment consideration, amendment
enactrnent or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making
proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding.
a Section82037 defines "legislative action" asthe drafting introduction, consideration,
modificatior; enacfinent or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment report nomination or other matter by
the Legislature orby either house or any committee, subcommittee, joint or select committee thereof, or by
a member or employee of the Legislature acting in his or her official capacity. "Legislative action" also
means the action of the Governor in approving orvetoing abill.


File No. A-01-254
Page No. 4
administrative agency does not include an appeararlce in a court of
law, before an administrative law judge, or before the Workers
Compensation Appeals Board. The prohibition of this paragraph
shall only apply to designated employees employed by a state
administrative agency on or after January 7 , 199I. " (Italics added.)
Subdivision (d)(2) of the same section provides:
"For purposes of [the above paragraph], a state administrative
agency of a designated employee of the Governor's office includes
any state administrative agency subject to the direction and control
of the Governor."
In regulationT8746.l, copy enclosed, the Commission sets forth a step-by-step
analytical framework with which to apply the prohibitions of section 87406. Each step is
discussed below as it applies to you in the order in which it appears in the regulation.
Q) fhe fficial has left his or her state ffice or employment, which means he or
she hqs either permanently left state service or is on a leave of absence. (Reg. 18746.1,
subd. (bXl).)
Your letter indicates you have left state service and are now an independent
communications consultant. Thus, this element is satisfied.
(2) The appeqrance or communication is madewithin 12 months after leaving
state ffice or employment. (Reg. 18746.1, subd. (bX2) )
Your letter indicates you left state service in mid-June200l. Insofar as you are
considering representing clients before the Power Authority before mid-2002, such
communications are potentially subject to these prohibitions.5
(3) The public fficial is compensated, or promised compensation, for the
appearance or communication. However, a payment madefor necessary travel, meals,
and accommodations received directly in connectionwithvoluntary services is not
prohibited or limited by this section. (Reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(3) )
Assuming, as above in the context of the discussion regarding lobbyist
registration requirements, you receive compensation for your efforts, this element will be
satisfied.
' The one year period coilrmences when an employee has permanenfly left state service and is no
longer receiving compensation, including compensation for unused vacation time. (Reg. 18746.1, subd.
@)(l); ,Yeil Advice Letter, No. A-97-247; Negrete Advice Letter, No. A-99-177.)


File No. A-01-254
Page No. 5
We have advised that restrictions on influencing administrative or legislative
action do not apply to paid or unpaid assistance rendered to a third person who
subsequently appears before or communicates with a former official's agency. Thus, the
ban of section 87406 did not restrict a former official from assisting or advising other
attorneys in the offtcial's law firm or clients themselves who might appear before or
communicate with the official's former agency regarding a regulation or legislation under
consideration. (Ordos Advice Letter, No. A-95-052.)
@) The appearance or communication is made on behalf of olty person as an
agent, attorney, or representative of that person. An appearance or communication made
by a public fficial solely to represent his or her personal interests, as defined in 2 Cal.
Code Regs. Section 18702.4, subdivision (b)(1), is not prohibited or limited by this
section. (Reg. 18746.1, subd. (b)(4) )
As your letter stipulates, you intend to represent potential clients before the Power
Authority. Thus, this element is satisfied.
(5) The appeorance or communication is madefor the purpose of influencing, as
defined in 2 Cal. Code Regs. Section 18746.2, any legislative or administrative action, or
any discretionary act involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a
permit, license, grant or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Reg.
r8746.1, subd. (b)(s) )
Your letter states the purpose of your representation is to obtain "power purchase
agreements" from the Power Authority. Insofar as these "agreements" are analogous to a
contract, this element is satisfied.o
We notg however, that communications with an agency that are not for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action are not restricted by section
87406. (Reg. 18746.2; Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. I-91-202; afact sheet is enclosed
for your assistance.)
(6) The appearance or communication is made before any fficer or employee of
any slate administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the Governor, if
the officialwas a designated employee of the Governor's offic, during the I2 months
before leaving state ffice or employment. (keg.18746.1, subd. (b)(6XC) )
Generally, a designated employee's state administrative agency7 means the
agency for which he or she worked, or any board or commission under the agency's
6 See Rrblic Utilities Code, section 3302, subdivision (d), defining "Bond purchase agreement" to
mean ".. . a contractual agreement executed between the authority and an underwriter or underwriters and,
where appropriate, a participating party, whereby the authority agrees to sell bonds issued pursuant to this
division."7 For purposes of section 87406, "state administrative agency" means every state office,
departrnent division, bureag board and commissio4 but does not include the Legislatme, the courts or any
agency in the judicial branch of govemment. ($ 87400.)


File No. A-01-254
Page No. 6
control. ($ 87a06; Reg. 18746.1, subd. (bX6XA), (B); Grimm Advice Lehter, No. I-96-
114; Gould Advice Letter, No. 4-96-077.) Section87406 does not prohibit every former
member of the executive branch from appearing before or communicatingwith the entire
executive branch. For example, in the Gould Advice Letter, supra, we advised that a
former director of the Department of Finance, a distinct state administrative agency, may
lobby administrative agencies in the executive branch, which he did not work for during
the course of his government service. In the course of his employment, we concluded
that he participated in the budget process as an advisor to the Governor and the
Legislature, but he did not work for or represent those agencies within the meaning of
section 87406, subdivision (d)(l) In making a determination as to which agency an
employee works for or represents, the scope of his or her duties is an important factor.
For ex-employees within the Governor's office, however, the restrictions are
much broader. Pursuant to the express language of the statute, the "state administrative
agency of a designated employee of the Governor's office includes any state
administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the Governor." ($ 87406,
subd. (d)(2).)8 Therefore, for ex-employees within the Governor's office, the primary
focus is on determining which agencies the Governor, not the employee, directs and
controls.e
Consistent with section87406, subdivision (d)(2), for ex-employees of the
Governor's office, we have advised that the "entire executive branch" is subject to the
direction and control of the Governor. (Zaremberg Advice Letter, No. A-92-038; Sybert
Advice Letter,No. I-93-380.)t0 This includes all state administrative agencies under the
direction and control of the Governor. The Power Authority is clearly a state
administrative agency within the meaning of section 87406. ($ 87400, subd (a) )
The Power Authority is, like other state administrative agencies, under the
direction and control of the Governor. The primary method of direction and control is
found in the Governor's power to appoint four of the five Power Authority board
members and appoint the chairperson of the board. (Pub. Util. Code $ 3325, subds. (a)(t)
and (d)(t).) While the other member of the board is the state treasurer, action may be
taken by the affirmative vote of only three board members. (Pub. Util. Code $ 3225,
subds. (a)(Z) and (c).) There is no provision in the enabling statutes that prevent the
Governor from replacing his appointed members at any time for any reason. Thus, the
t The legislative history pertinent to section 87406 does not illuminate the meaning of "direction
and control."
' Your letter implies that you believe the Power Autlrority did not exist during your tenure in the
Governor's office. While our conclusion is not influenced by this facq we note for the record that
legislation creating the Power Authority was signed by the Governor on May L6,2007, approximate$ one
monthbefore your departure. (Stats. 2001, Ch. 10.)
'o See Commission staffmemorandum regarding adoption of regulation 18746.1 arrd 18746.2,
December 22, 1998, interpreting subdivision (bX6)(C) to mean: "To the entire executive branch, if the
employee was a desiglated employee of the Governor's office during the one-year period. (Subdivision
G)(6XC)."


File No. A-01-254
Page No. 7
Power Authority is "subject" to the direction and control of the Governor by virtue of
these powers.
Consequently, for one year after you left the Governor's office, you may not, for
compensation, act as representative or agentfor any person before the Governor's office
or any state administrative agemcy subject to the direction and control of the Governor,
including the Power Authority, for the purpose of influencing administrative or
legislative action, or any action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment,
awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant, or contrac! or the sale or purchase of
goods or property. ($ 87406.)
If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916)
322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General
By:
C. Scott Tocher
Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosures

2. Does the one-year prohibition apply to the Governor's former communications
Director even though the Power Authority did not exist at the time he was employed?
3. Is the Power Authority under the direction and control of the Governor's
office?
sections.conclusion:
1. No. Your activities in securing contracts do not comprise an attempt to
influence legislative or administrative action.
All citations are to the Governmerfi Code unless
indicated otherwise.


File No A-01-254
2. Yes
3. Yes. The Power Authority is a state administrative agency subject to the
direction and control of the Governor.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: 1. Does activity involved in seeking Power Purchase Agreements constitute
lobbying requiring registration as a lobbyist, or is it in the nature of procurement that
does not necessitate registration as a lobbyist?
2. Does the one-year prohibition apply to the Governor's former communications
Director even though the Power Authority did not exist at the time he was employed?
3. Is the Power Authority under the direction and control of the Governor's
office?

CONCLUSION: 1. No. Your activities in securing contracts do not comprise an attempt to
influence legislative or administrative action.
All citations are to the Governmerfi Code unless
indicated otherwise.


File No A-01-254
2. Yes
3. Yes. The Power Authority is a state administrative agency subject to the
direction and control of the Governor.

Box 807 . 428 J Street o Sacramento, CA 95812-0807 (916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886 December 19,2001 Phil Trounstine 620 Middlefield Drive Aptos, CA 95003 Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-01-254 Dear Mr. Trounstine This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").l This letter should not be construed as advice on any conduct thatmay have already taken place. Finally, our response is based on the facts presented. The Commission does not act as a finder of fact in its advice-giving capacity. Qn re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) QUESTTON 1. Does activity involved in seeking Power Purchase Agreements constitute lobbying requiring registration as a lobbyist, or is it in the nature of procurement that does not necessitate registration as a lobbyist? 2. Does the one-year prohibition apply to the Governor's former communications Director even though the Power Authority did not exist at the time he was employed? 3. Is the Power Authority under the direction and control of the Governor's office? CONCLUSION 1. No. Your activities in securing contracts do not comprise an attempt to influence legislative or administrative action. I Government Code sections 81000- 91014. Commissionregulations appearatTitle 2, sections 18109-18997, of tlte California Code of Regulations. All citations are to the Governmerfi Code unless indicated otherwise. File No A-01-254 Page No. 2 2. Yes 3. Yes. The Power Authority is a state administrative agency subject to the direction and control of the Governor. F'ACTS You were communications director for Governor Gray Davis from January 1999 through mid-June 2001. You are now an independent communications consultant. You have recently been asked by a potential client to represent their interest in obtaining power purchase agreements from the California Consumer Power and Conservation Financing Authority ("Power Authority"). ANALYSIS I. Lobbvist Status Section 82039 defines a lobbyist as "'Lobbyist'means any individual who receives two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more in economic consideration in a calendar month, other than reimbursement for reasonable travel expenses, or whose principal duties as an employee are to communicate directly or through his or her agents with any elective state official, agemcy official, or legislative offrcial for the purpose of influencing legislative or administrative action. No individual is a lobbyist by reason of activities described in Section 86300." "Influencing legislative or administrative action" is defined as "communicating directly or taking any other action for the principal purpose of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing arly legislative or administrative action." (Reg 18239, subd. (d)(a).) We assume, forpurposes of our analysis here, that the Power Authority is a state agency. "Administrative action" is defined in section 82002 as the proposal, drafting, development, consideration, amendment, enactment or defeat by any state agency of any rule, regulation or other action in any rate-making proceeding or any quasi-legislative proceeding.
